paper_title: BioMatrix: Towards a Comprehensive Biological Foundation Model Spanning the Modality Matrix of Sequences, Structures, and Language

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: We present BioMatrix, the first multimodal foundation model that natively integrates sequences, structures, and natural language for both molecules and proteins within a single decoder-only architecture. Existing biological foundation models pursue native multimodality and broad entity coverage separately: those that fuse multiple modalities under a shared objective remain confined to a single entity type, while those spanning multiple entity types either omit explicit structural modeling or rely on adapter-based designs in which the model cannot natively generate the very modalities it can read. BioMatrix closes this gap by mapping molecular sequences (supporting both SMILES and SELFIES notations), molecular structures, protein sequences, protein structures, and natural language into a shared discrete token space through a unified tokenization scheme, so that all modalities are consumed and produced uniformly under a single next-token prediction objective -- without external encoders, projection adapters, or modality-specific output heads. Built upon the Qwen3 language model (1.7B and 4B), BioMatrix is continually pretrained on 304.4 billion tokens spanning general and domain-specific text, sequence and structure views of molecules and proteins, and cross-modal corpora that interleave biomolecular entities with scientific text and link distinct entities through molecule-protein and protein-protein interaction data. After tuning on a comprehensive suite of downstream applications covering 80 tasks across 6 categories -- encompassing single-entity and multi-entity understanding and generation tasks across and within modalities -- BioMatrix achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance on 77 out of 80 tasks, demonstrating that a single, natively multimodal generalist model can effectively match or surpass specialized approaches across a wide range of biological tasks.

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: Foundation models trained on large-scale data have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing and beyond, demonstrating strong capabilities in reasoning, generation, and cross-task generalization [9, 142, 39, 155]. Extending this paradigm to the biological and chemical sciences holds transformative potential for drug discovery, protein engineering, and molecular design [150, 151, 97]. Biological entities, however, are not defined by single representation. molecule or protein may be specified as sequence, realized as spatial structure, and interpreted through natural-language descriptions of function, activity, disease relevance, or experimental context. These views play complementary roles: sequences provide compact symbolic identity, structures determine physical interaction and function, and language carries both human design intent and the accumulated knowledge of the literature. Biological tasks also rarely involve one entity in isolation; drug discovery, protein engineering, and mechanism understanding routinely depend on moleculeprotein and proteinprotein relationships. general biological foundation model should therefore support both native multimodalitytreating sequence, structure, and language as first-class inputs and outputs of the same generative modeland broad entity coverage across molecules, proteins, and their interactions. Despite rapid progress on individual fronts, no existing model meets both requirements at once. Existing efforts toward such unified model can be organized along two largely independent axes: how biomolecular content is coupled with natural language, and how broadly different biomolecular entity types are covered. Progress along each axis has so far been pursued in isolation, and the two have yet to be reconciled within single architecture. Axis 1: coupling biomolecular content with natural language. Work along this axis broadly falls into two paradigms. The adapter-based paradigm keeps domain encoders and language model as separate components, as in BioMedGPT [79] and 3D-MoLM [63]. While this paradigm benefits from specialized encoders, it introduces an inputoutput asymmetry: biomolecular content enters the LLM as continuous encoder features but must be emitted through the LLMs text vocabulary, making native generation of non-textual modalities such as molecular or protein structures difficult without external decoders. The native-tokenization paradigm instead discretizes biomolecular content into tokens consumed under the same next-token objective as language, mirroring the shift from late-fusion adapters to early-fusion token streams in visionlanguage modeling [120, 21, 50]. In biology, this direction is enabled by line notations such as SMILES, SELFIES, and amino acid strings, as well as recent SE(3)-invariant geometry tokenizers for molecules [32] and proteins [42, 124, 114, 99]. Existing models in this paradigm remain partial: BioT5 [95, 96], NatureLM [137], and SciReasoner [132] unify sequences with text but omit explicit structure, while ESM3 [42] includes sequence and structure but covers only proteins. Axis 2: covering multiple biomolecular entity types. parallel line of work pursues unified modeling across biomolecular entities themselves, often without natural language. AlphaFold3 [4] jointly represents proteins, nucleic acids, and molecules for structure prediction, and ODesign [149] extends this direction to all-atom generative design. More recently, LOGOS [61] from the Tongyi team broadens the scope further by encoding diverse scientific objects under shared grammar, including proteins, antibodies, small molecules, reactions, materials, pockets, and proteinligand complexes. This grammar-based formulation is complementary to our goal: it emphasizes symbolic scientific object descriptions and contact/constraint patterns, whereas BioMatrix unifies natural language, biomolecular sequences, and explicit structure tokens in single decoder-only next-token interface. Overall, these models show that broad cross-entity coverage is feasible, but their token spaces and objectives remain largely separate from language-grounded prediction over both sequence and structure. Consequently, design intents must be specified through structured configurations rather than functional descriptions, and the models cannot directly leverage the textual knowledge available in biological literature. sections.title: Protein
sections.content: 1D Seq Text 3D Struct NL single entity type, while models that span multiple entity types either lack natural language or omit explicit structural modeling. With mature sequence, text, and SE(3)-invariant geometry tokenizers now available, the remaining challenge is integration: reconciling heterogeneous tokenizers into one shared vocabulary and curating supervision dense enough to bind sequences, structures, language, and cross-entity relationships under unified next-token objective. We close this gap with BioMatrix, multimodal foundation model that, to our knowledge, is the first to natively integrate sequence, structure, and natural language for both molecules and proteins within single decoder-only architecture. Built upon Qwen3 [142] (1.7B and 4B), BioMatrix serializes all supported modalities into one discrete token stream: SMILES and SELFIES for molecular sequences, SELFIES-aligned tokens for molecular conformations, residue-level sequence tokens and GCP-VQVAE structure tokens for proteins, and the original language-model vocabulary for natural language. Modality-specific boundary tokens allow these segments to be freely interleaved, so tasks that are usually architecturally distinctcaptioning, text-conditioned design, folding, inverse folding, structure generation, and interaction predictionbecome different conditional generation patterns under one next-token objective. Several design choices make this interface practical rather than merely conceptual. For molecules, we adapt MolStrucTok [32] with branch-decoupled decoder and compose frequent SELFIESstructure pairs into joint tokens, binding chemical identity to local geometry without exploding the vocabulary. For proteins, we retain factored representation between residue tokens and GCP-VQVAE [99] structure tokens, preserving residue-level sequencestructure alignment while avoiding prohibitively large joint residuestructure vocabulary. To train the resulting model, we construct 304.4-billion-token corpus spanning scientific text, moleculeand protein-centric multi-view data, interleaved biomedical text, and moleculeprotein/proteinprotein interaction data, using stochastic composition and protein three-view instances to expose both isolated and jointly grounded modality views. We evaluate the resulting backbone after instruction tuning on 80 tasks across 6 categories, covering molecular generation and prediction, moleculetext translation, molecular conformer generation, protein understanding and design, protein structure tasks, and biomolecular interaction prediction. BioMatrix achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) or competitive performance on 77 out of 80 tasks, with the largest gains on settings that exercise the unified design, including text-based molecule generation, property-conditioned molecular conformer generation, protein sequencestructure co-generation, and structure-grounded moleculeprotein affinity prediction. Our main contributions are as follows:

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Figure 1: Overview of BioMatrix. Natural language, molecular line notations (SMILES, SELFIES), molecular structures, protein sequences, and protein structures are mapped into shared discrete token space via unified tokenization scheme, processed by single Qwen3-based decoder backbone under next-token prediction, and decoded back to their respective modalities through modality-specific de-tokenizers. unified architecture along both axes. We propose BioMatrix, the first foundation model that simultaneously achieves native multimodality (sequences, structures, and natural language) and broad entity coverage (molecules, proteins, and their interactions) within single decoder-only architecture, supporting both singleand multi-entity understanding and generation under one nexttoken prediction objective. unified tokenization scheme. We design tokenization scheme that consolidates molecular sequences (in both SMILES and SELFIES), protein sequences, molecular and protein structures, and natural language into single shared discrete vocabulary, enabling heterogeneous biomolecular inputs and outputs to be handled uniformly without external encoders or projection adapters. Empirical evidence at scale. We curate 304.4-billion-token pretraining corpus spanning withinmodality and cross-modality data, and instruction-tune across 80 tasks in 6 categories. Built on single unified pretrained backbone, BioMatrix matches or surpasses specialized baselines on 77 out of 80 tasks, providing the first empirical evidence that the two axes above can be unified without compromising per-task performance.

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Figure 2: 3D structure tokenization pipelines in BioMatrix. The molecule structure tokenizer converts each 3D conformer into SELFIES-aligned joint 1D3D tokens through local spherical descriptors and 512-entry vector-quantized codebook, while the protein structure tokenizer encodes backbone geometry into per-residue structure tokens using GCPNet-based encoder and 4096-entry codebook. Dashed arrows indicate the reconstruction paths used by the corresponding decoders.

sections.title: 2 Unified Multimodal Tokenization
sections.content: A central design goal of BioMatrix is to process sequences, structures, and natural language for both molecules and proteins within single decoder-only model. To this end, we adopt unified tokenization scheme that maps all modalities into shared discrete token space, so that heterogeneous biomolecular inputs can be handled by the same next-token prediction objective. Concretely, natural language and sequence-like representations of molecules (SMILES, SELFIES) and proteins (amino acid sequences) are tokenized as ordinary token sequences, while molecular conformations and protein backbones are discretized into structural codes by two dedicated structure tokenizers: we build on MolStrucTok [32] for molecules (Section 2.1, where we additionally propose branch-decoupled decoder) and directly adopt GCP-VQVAE [99] for protein backbones (Section 2.2). Figure 2 summarizes these two structure-tokenization pipelines. These structural codes, together with the sequence and language tokens, are then integrated into single extended Qwen3 vocabulary alongside modality-specific control tokens that delimit each modality segment (Section 2.3). Finally, the embeddings of all newly added tokens are initialized through description-based scheme (Section 2.4).

sections.title: 2.1 Molecule Structure Tokenizer
sections.content: For molecular structures, we adopt MolStrucTok [32] as our structure tokenizer, which discretizes continuous atomic coordinates into compact set of SE(3)-invariant structural codes that can be seamlessly consumed by language model. We briefly review the components relevant to BioMatrix here, and refer readers to the original paper for full architectural and training details. Compared to the original design, we make two modifications tailored to our setting: we drop the understanding descriptors (bond lengths and bond angles to nearest neighbors) and retain only the generation descriptors, which we find sufficient for faithful structure reconstruction; and we replace the original monolithic decoder with branch-decoupled variant that disentangles the heterogeneous geometric targets and

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: noticeably improves reconstruction fidelity. We describe both changes alongside the corresponding components below. Geometric input. MolStrucTok represents molecular conformer as an augmented line notation in which every atom token is paired with local geometric descriptor on top of its chemical identity. Following the order induced by the underlying SELFIES sequence, each atom vi is assigned local spherical coordinate frame anchored at focal atom and two reference atoms, all selected via 2D topological proximity to vi. Within this frame, the position of vi is encoded by three scalars: the radial distance di to the focal atom, the polar angle θi, and the azimuthal angle φi. Because the frame is constructed purely from intrinsic graph topology and relative geometry, the resulting descriptors are inherently invariant to global translation and rotation. Since θi [0, π] and φi (π, π], to normalize their ranges, φi is further decomposed into its absolute value and sign, yielding four-dimensional input gi = (di, θi, abs(φi), sign(φi)). (1) As noted above, we omit the understanding features (bond lengths and bond angles to the four nearest neighbors) used in the original MolStrucTok and keep only these generation descriptors gi. Encoding pipeline. The continuous descriptors gi are discretized by VQ-VAE [123]. After lognormalizing the length component and rescaling the angular components to unit range, an MLP encoder maps each gi into latent embedding, which is then assigned to its nearest entry in learnable codebook of size 512. The selected code index serves as the structural token for atom vi, yielding per-atom sequence of discrete geometric codes whose order follows that of the underlying SELFIES. Decoding pipeline and branch-decoupled decoder. At decoding time, each codebook entry is passed through decoder that reconstructs the four-dimensional descriptor gi, from which 3D coordinates are recovered by sequentially traversing the molecule and reconstructing each local spherical frame. The original MolStrucTok decoder regresses all four components with shared trunk followed by single output head. We observe, however, that the four entries of gi are heterogeneous in both physical meaning and numerical scaledi is positive length, θi and abs(φi) are bounded angles, and sign(φi) is binary indicatorand that forcing shared trunk to jointly regress all of them introduces interference among these targets and limits reconstruction accuracy. We therefore replace the monolithic decoder with branch-decoupled variant: the shared trunk is removed, and each of the four targetslength, polar angle, azimuthal magnitude, and azimuthal signis predicted by its own dedicated MLP head operating directly on the quantized code. symmetric modification on the encoder side yielded no measurable benefit, suggesting that the bottleneck lies in disentangling heterogeneous outputs rather than in encoding them. With this change alone, the average percomponent RMSD improves by roughly 0.1 on QM9-2014 [101] over the original tokenizer, while leaving the codebook size, encoder, and overall token interface unchanged. Integration into BioMatrix. For each molecule, we run the MolStrucTok encoder over its conformation to obtain per-atom sequence of discrete structural codes drawn from the 512-entry codebook, aligned one-to-one with the corresponding SELFIES atom tokens. Their integration into BioMatrixs joint vocabulary is described in Section 2.3.

sections.title: 2.2 Protein Structure Tokenizer
sections.content: For protein structures, we adopt GCP-VQVAE [99] as-is, without architectural modification, since it already offers strong reconstruction fidelity and generalization to unseen proteins among publicly available open-source structure tokenizers at comparable codebook sizes. Specifically, we use its Large variant throughout BioMatrix. Conceptually, GCP-VQVAE plays the same role for proteins

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: that MolStrucTok plays for molecules: it converts continuous backbone into sequence of discrete, pose-invariant structural symbols, and provides decoder that maps those symbols back to backbone coordinates. The key difference is the unit of tokenization. Rather than assigning one code per atom, GCP-VQVAE assigns one code per residue, so protein chain of length becomes length-L sequence of structural tokens aligned with the amino acid sequence. Geometric input. GCP-VQVAE operates on protein backbone coordinates, taking the (N, Cα, C) atoms of each residue as input. Each residue is treated as node in protein graph, with geometric features derived from the relative positions and orientations of neighboring backbone frames rather than from absolute Cartesian coordinates. This is important because structure token should describe the local fold of residue, not the arbitrary global rotation or translation of the protein in coordinate file. By restricting itself to backbone geometry, the tokenizer captures the global fold and local secondary-structure arrangement of protein while remaining agnostic to side-chain conformations, which keeps the discrete vocabulary compact and broadly applicable to both experimentally resolved and predicted structures. Encoding pipeline. The backbone graph is first processed by strictly SE(3)-equivariant GCPNet encoder [88]. GCPNet maintains scalar features together with vector features, and performs message passing in local geometric frames so that the representation can retain directional and chiral information while remaining well behaved under global rigid motions. The resulting per-residue embeddings summarize each residues local backbone environment as well as information propagated from nearby residues. transformer encoder then refines these residue embeddings with global sequence-level context. Finally, vector-quantization layer replaces each continuous residue embedding with the nearest entry in learnable codebook of size 4,096. The selected code index is the protein structure token for that residue. Thus the encoder maps {(xN , xCα , xC )}L i=1 (z1, . . . , zL), zi {1, . . . , 4,096}, (2) where each zi is discrete summary of the backbone geometry around residue i. Decoding pipeline. The decoder performs the inverse operation. Each discrete code zi is first mapped back to its corresponding codebook vector, producing sequence of continuous embeddings with the same length as the protein chain. transformer decoder then predicts rigid frame for each residue, parameterized by translation and rotation represented with continuous 6D rotation head. These predicted residue frames are applied to an idealized local backbone template containing the N, Cα, and atoms, yielding reconstructed backbone coordinates for the full chain. In this sense, the code sequence does not directly store Cartesian coordinates; it stores enough local geometric information for the decoder to recover globally coherent backbone after the residue-level frames are assembled. Training uses reconstruction losses on the recovered coordinates and geometry together with the standard VQ codebook and commitment objectives, so the learned codes form compact geometric vocabulary that can be generated autoregressively by BioMatrix. Integration into BioMatrix. For each protein, we run the GCP-VQVAE Large encoder over its backbone structure to obtain length-L sequence of discrete per-residue structural tokens drawn from the 4,096-entry codebook, one token per residue. Because this sequence is residue-aligned, it can be paired directly with the dedicated amino-acid tokens introduced in Section 2.3: folding becomes generation of z1, . . . , zL from amino-acid tokens, while inverse folding becomes generation of aminoacid tokens from z1, . . . , zL. At output time, generated structure-token sequences are passed through the GCP-VQVAE decoder to recover backbone coordinates. Their integration into BioMatrixs joint vocabulary is described in Section 2.3.

sections.title: 2.3 Vocabulary Extension
sections.content: Category Modality Example 1D SMILES <mol smi start> # CC # <mol smi end> Molecule 1D SELFIES <mol sfi start> [C] [#C] [C] [#N] <mol sfi end> 3D Structure <mol 3d start> [H 3] [C 0] [#C 6] [C 132] [#N 497] <mol 3d end> Protein 1D AA Sequence <prot aa start> <A M> <A R> <A A> <A K> <A W> <prot aa end> 3D Structure <prot 3d start> <S 4012> <S 153> <S 2091> <S 2440> <S 3117> <prot 3d end> Table 2: Examples of wrapped sequences for each modality in the extended vocabulary. Each molecule or protein segment is delimited by dedicated pair of start/end special tokens, while natural language text is left unwrapped and serves as the default carrier modality. Each light-blue box corresponds to single token produced by the tokenizer on the wrapped segment. Molecular 3D tokens are formed by composing each SELFIES token with its 3D code from MolStrucTok [32]; protein 3D tokens form standalone set produced by GCP-VQVAE [99]. To accommodate the full spectrum of biomolecular modalities within single decoder-only model, we extend the original Qwen3 vocabulary along two orthogonal axes: modality-specific tokens that encode molecular and protein sequence and structure representations, and structural control tokens that delimit each modality segment within mixed-modality sequence. Table 2 summarizes the full set of additions, which we describe below. 1D tokens. For natural language, we directly reuse the Qwen3 tokenizer without modification. For molecules, SMILES strings are tokenized directly by the Qwen3 tokenizer since SMILES is widely used line notation that Qwen3 has already been exposed to during pretraining. SELFIES, in contrast, is more recent and less prevalent notation whose bracketed atomic symbols are not natively recognized, and we add them as new vocabulary entries. We retain both SMILES and SELFIES as parallel molecular line notations rather than committing to single one since our downstream experiments (Section 6.1) reveal that the two notations exhibit complementary strengths. For proteins, we introduce dedicated vocabulary of 26 residue-level tokens covering the 20 standard amino acids together with non-standard and unknown residue symbols. Although amino acid letters already lie within Qwen3s character set, its tokenizer merges them into variable-length subword pieces, which would break the one-to-one correspondence between residues and the per-residue 3D tokens produced by GCP-VQVAE (Section 2.2). Enforcing single-token-per-residue encoding preserves this alignment and directly supports downstream tasks that operate on residue-level sequencestructure correspondences, such as folding (mapping each AA token to its structural token) and inverse folding (the reverse direction). 3D tokens. The molecular and protein structure vocabularies are inherited from the two structure tokenizers introduced in Sections 2.1 and 2.2, but we integrate them into BioMatrixs vocabulary in two different ways, guided by how many joint sequencestructure entries each entity type would actually need. For molecules, we compose each MolStrucTok code with its aligned SELFIES atom token into joint entry, following the original MolStrucTok design, so that every structure-tokenized molecule token simultaneously carries chemical identity and local geometry. In principle, pairing the 925 unique SELFIES atom tokens observed in our pretraining corpus with the 512 MolStrucTok codes could yield up to 470K combinations, but in practice the vast majority never occur. We therefore enumerate joint tokens from the pretraining data in descending order of frequency and greedily retain

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: the smallest prefix that covers more than 99% of occurrences, which yields 11,294 joint entries. This composed vocabulary is compact enough to be learned reliably while keeping tail combinations, whose embeddings would be under-trained, out of the vocabulary. For proteins, we keep the 4,096 GCP-VQVAE codes as standalone set of per-residue structure tokens, parallel to the 26 residue-level amino-acid tokens (standard amino acids plus non-standard and unknown symbols). Applying the same 99%-coverage procedure to AAstructure pairs would require 71,737 joint entries (nearly half the size of the original Qwen3 vocabulary, 151,669), and long tail of these combinations occurs only handful of times in the pretraining corpus, leaving their embeddings severely under-trained. We therefore opt for factored representation in which each residue is described by an AA token and structure token independently. Structural control tokens. To let the model distinguish heterogeneous content within single token stream, we further introduce five pairs of start/end special tokens that mark the boundaries of each non-text biomolecular segment (rightmost column of Table 2). Natural language is left unwrapped and serves as the default carrier modality, while molecular and protein segments, whether sequenceor structure-based, are explicitly enclosed by their corresponding control-token pair. This design provides clean interface between continuous prose and structured biomolecular content, and allows text, molecular representations, and protein representations to be interleaved within one training instance under BioMatrixs unified next-token prediction framework.

sections.title: 2.4 Embedding Initialization
sections.content: Extending Qwen3s vocabulary with the modality-specific tokens and structural control tokens introduced in Section 2.3 adds several thousand new entries to both the input embedding table and, when not tied, the output projection. Initializing these new rows from scratch (e.g., Gaussian noise) discards the well-trained semantic geometry of the underlying language model and slows down the early stages of continual pretraining. We therefore adopt simple description-based initialization scheme that grounds every newly added token in the pretrained embedding space of Qwen3 itself. Description-based initialization. For each new token t, we first construct short natural-language description desc(t) that summarizes its semantic role. The description is then tokenized by the original Qwen3 tokenizer into sequence of subword ids (s1, . . . , sk), and the new embedding is initialized as the mean of the corresponding pretrained input embeddings, plus small isotropic Gaussian perturbation: et = 1 j=1 EQwen3[sj] + ϵ, ϵ (cid:16) (cid:17) , 0, 1 (3) where EQwen3 denotes the pretrained Qwen3 input embedding matrix and is the embedding dimension. The noise term breaks symmetry between tokens whose descriptions happen to share subword ids, while keeping each new embedding within the typical scale of the pretrained vocabulary. Per-category descriptions. The description desc(t) is constructed in category-specific manner that reflects how much prior semantic content each token actually carries: Structural control tokens are mapped to short phrases describing the modality boundary they mark, e.g., <mol smi start> Begins molecule SMILES string, <prot 3d end> Ends protein 3D structure. This grounds each delimiter in the same semantic space as the surrounding natural-language context. Protein 1D tokens are mapped to the full English name of their corresponding amino acid (e.g., <A A> Alanine, <A W> Tryptophan), with non-standard or ambiguous symbols mapped to descriptive phrases such as Selenocysteine or any amino acid.

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Molecular 1D, molecular 3D, and protein 3D tokens have no informative natural-language glossa SELFIES atom token or VQ codebook index is essentially an opaque symbol. For these categories, we use the raw token string as its own description, which yields near-uniform initialization centered on the subword decomposition of the token literal while still benefiting from the noise term to break ties. Discussion. This scheme requires no additional training or auxiliary models: it reuses the frozen Qwen3 embedding table as fixed semantic encoder for the description strings, and writes the resulting vectors directly into the expanded embedding rows before continual pretraining begins. In our preliminary experiments, we find that this description-based initialization provides more stable starting point than random initialization in the early phase of continual pretraining.

sections.title: 3 Multimodal Continual Pretraining
sections.content: To equip BioMatrix with joint understanding over sequences, structures, and language for both molecules and proteins, we perform continual pretraining on large-scale, heterogeneous corpus that spans general and scientific text, molecular and protein data with both sequence and structure views, and cross-entity resources linking distinct biomolecular entities or wrapping them into free-form scientific text. All modalities are serialized into shared token space via the unified tokenization scheme described in Section 2, with each modality segment explicitly delimited by the modality-specific start/end tokens. In this section, we first describe the composition and curation of the pretraining corpus (Section 3.1), and then report the training dynamics of BioMatrix under this data mixture (Section 3.2).

sections.title: 3.1 Pretraining Corpus
sections.content: We organize the corpus into four categories: (i) generaland scientific-domain text (Section 3.1.1); (ii) molecule-centric data, where within-molecule sequence, structure, and textual views are jointly exposed (Section 3.1.2); (iii) protein-centric data, organized analogously to the molecular case (Section 3.1.3); and (iv) cross-entity and interleaved data that link distinct biomolecular entities or wrap them into scientific prose (Section 3.1.4). For molecular and protein data, we adopt two complementary strategies that operate at different levels of instance construction: stochastic composition governs how fields are sampled and ordered within single instance, while the three-view instance pattern controls how multiple instances are emitted from the same biomolecular entity. Stochastic composition applies to every moleculeand protein-centric instance. For each instance, the inclusion of optional fields is controlled by predefined probabilities, and the ordering of interchangeable components (e.g., property fields, annotation entries, or co-present modalities) is randomly permuted across samples. For example, single PubChem molecule may yield an instance that randomly samples subset of SMILES, SELFIES, tokenized conformation, IUPAC name, textual description, physicochemical properties and permutes their order. This design prevents the model from overfitting to any fixed input schema and encourages it to jointly learn associations among heterogeneous representations [90]. The three-view instance pattern is applied specifically to protein-centric data sources that provide both sequences and structures. For each such protein, we emit (i) sequence-only instance containing the amino acid sequence, (ii) structure-only instance containing the tokenized backbone, and (iii) joint instance containing both modalities with their relative order randomly permuted, exposing the model to both isolated and jointly grounded views of each protein. Protein data sources providing only sequences emit the sequence view alone; sources that additionally carry functional annotations further emit separate composite instance that combines those annotations with the available modalities under the stochastic composition strategy. We apply this pattern to proteins because sequence and structure provide largely complementary, residue-level-

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Figure 3: Overview of the continual pretraining corpus composition. Token budget distribution across four data categories general and scientific text, molecule-centric data, protein-centric data, and cross-entity/interleaved data spanning sequence, structure, and textual modalities, totaling 304.4 billion tokens. aligned views of the same entity, and we want the model to handle each modality both independently and jointly. In total, our pretraining corpus comprises approximately 304.4 billion tokens, with text and protein data contributing the largest shares, followed by molecular and cross-entity data. Figure 3 summarizes the full token budget across categories and modalities.

sections.title: 3.1.1 Text Corpora
sections.content: General-domain text. We use FineWeb-Edu [76], large-scale, education-oriented filtered subset of CommonCrawl, as our source of general-domain natural language, which helps prevent catastrophic forgetting of the models general language abilities during continual pretraining. Scientific-domain text. To strengthen the models scientific language proficiency, we curate text from two complementary sources. From FineFineWeb [81], domain-categorized web corpus, we select the four subsets most relevant to our target disciplines: biology, chemistry, medicine, and health. To complement web-sourced text with more rigorous scientific writing, we additionally extract large collection of full-article texts from PubMed [135], covering peer-reviewed literature in molecular biology, pharmacology, and clinical research.

sections.title: 3.1.2 Molecular Data
sections.content: PubChem. We collect all compounds available in PubChem [54] as of December 2025 and construct multi-view training instances that jointly expose the model to structure, properties, and annotations. For each molecule, we extract up to three representationscanonical SMILES, SELFIES, and tokenized conformation produced by our molecular structure tokenizer (Section 2.1)together with available physicochemical properties (e.g., molecular weight, hydrogen bond donor/acceptor counts, topological polar surface area, XLogP3) and textual annotations such as IUPAC names and natural language descriptions. These heterogeneous components are concatenated into single training instance following the stochastic composition strategy described above. Since PubChems conformer coverage

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: is limited and its conformations are generated by lightweight force fields, we further incorporate DFT-level conformations from PCQM4Mv2 [45] and PubChemQC [83], processed through the same structure tokenizer and merged into the corpus under the same composition strategy. MolTextNet. To strengthen moleculetext alignment, we incorporate MolTextNet [154], corpus of approximately 2.5M moleculedescription pairs derived from ChEMBL35 [148], whose descriptions synthesized by GPT-4o-mini from structural, physicochemical, bioactivity, and synthesizability annotations are substantially longer and more informative than prior moleculetext resources. For each entry, we canonicalize the SMILES, derive the corresponding SELFIES, and assemble the three modalities into single training instance under the same stochastic composition strategy.

sections.title: 3.1.3 Protein Data
sections.content: UniRef50. We include protein sequences from UniRef50 [118] to provide broad coverage of the protein sequence space. RCSB PDB. We incorporate experimentally determined protein structures from the RCSB Protein Data Bank [13]. For each chain, we extract the amino acid sequence and discretize its backbone geometry into structural tokens via our protein structure tokenizer (Section 2.2), then apply the three-view instance pattern. UniProt/Swiss-Prot. We use the manually curated Swiss-Prot subset of UniProt [1] as our primary source of annotated proteins, pairing each entry with its predicted structure from the latest release of the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB) [14] when available. Beyond sequence and structure, Swiss-Prot provides rich functional metadata: protein name, description, organism, taxonomic lineage, subcellular localization, keywords, and free-text comments on function, catalytic activity, and biological context. For each protein, we serialize these annotations together with the available sequence and structure views into composite training instance, and additionally emit the three-view instance pattern so that the model is exposed to both isolated and jointly grounded views. UniProt/TrEMBL. To scale up coverage of the protein sequencefunction space, we further include the automatically annotated TrEMBL subset of UniProt [1]. As most TrEMBL entries lack resolved or predicted structures at our processing time, we retain only the amino acid sequence together with its functional annotations, with evidence codes and literature citations removed. TrEMBL provides broad taxonomic and functional coverage that complements the smaller but more richly grounded Swiss-Prot samples. AlphaFold Protein Structure Database. Beyond the Swiss-Prot predictions already paired with UniProt annotations, we incorporate two additional curated subsets from the AFDB [14]: the model organism proteomes (covering 16 reference species including human, mouse, E. coli, and S. cerevisiae) and the global health proteomes (covering pathogens relevant to neglected tropical and infectious diseases). To further scale structural diversity, we additionally sample approximately 130M predicted structures from the full AFDB, representing over 60% of all entries in the database and covering substantial fraction of the known protein universe. All entries are processed through the same structure tokenizer and emit the three-view instance pattern.

sections.title: 3.1.4 Cross-Entity and Interleaved Data
sections.content: While Sections 3.1.2 and 3.1.3 already expose the model to within-entity cross-modal alignment (e.g., molecule paired with its own conformation and description), this section focuses on data that link distinct biomolecular entities to each other or wrap them into free-form scientific text.

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Interleaved biomedical text. To encourage tight cross-modal grounding between natural language and biomolecular representations, we construct an interleaved corpus in which biomedical entities mentioned in scientific text are directly paired with their structural representations. Source documents are drawn from four complementary corporaPubMed abstracts [135], bioRxiv abstracts [109], S2ORC [74], and the USPTO-Applications patent corpus [19, 75]jointly covering biomedical literature, preprints, broad scientific publications, and chemical patents. We apply BERN2 [117], neural biomedical named entity recognition and normalization tool, to detect mentions of molecules and proteins and link them to canonical database identifiers. For each recognized entity, we retrieve the corresponding structural representationsSMILES and SELFIES for molecules, amino acid sequences for proteinsand append them inline after the entity mention, wrapped with the appropriate special tokens. This exposes the model to natural language contexts in which textual descriptions co-occur with their underlying structural representations, facilitating fine-grained alignment between biomedical terminology and biomolecular entities. Biomolecular interaction data. To equip the model with knowledge of biomolecular interactions, we incorporate curated interaction datasets spanning both moleculeprotein and proteinprotein pairs in sequence-only and structure-grounded forms. For moleculeprotein interactions, we collect sequence-level pairs from BindingDB [69], STITCH [89], and the jglaser binding affinity dataset [34], and structure-grounded complexes from CrossDocked2020 [31]. For proteinprotein interactions, we use AlphaSeq [2] as the sequence-level source and PPIRef [17] as the structure-grounded source.

sections.title: 3.2 Training Dynamics
sections.content: We conduct continual pretraining on top of the LLaMA-Factory [152] framework. We train both BioMatrix-1.7B and BioMatrix-4B on 64 NVIDIA H100 GPUs with global batch size of 1,024 and maximum sequence length of 8,192 tokens, using the AdamW optimizer with peak learning rate of 2.0 104 under cosine schedule with 2,000 warmup steps. Training runs for approximately 36.4K steps (1 epoch), consuming the full 304.4B-token corpus described in Section 3.1. The resulting training loss curves are shown in Figure 4. On loss spikes. closer inspection of the curves reveals several transient loss spikes for both model sizes, concentrated primarily in the early-to-middle phase of training. In all cases, the loss recovers to its pre-spike trajectory within small number of steps without any manual intervention (e.g., learning-rate rewinding or optimizer-state rollback). To assess whether these spikes cause any latent degradation that is not visible in the training loss itself, we evaluated checkpoints before and after each prominent spike on held-out validation set. Across all evaluated spikes, we observe no meaningful degradation: preand post-spike checkpoints achieve essentially comparable validation loss, and subsequent checkpoints continue to improve smoothly as training proceeds. Given that the diversity of modalities inherently increases the risk of optimization instability, we interpret the observed spikes as localized optimization perturbations (most likely triggered by occasional high-gradient batches arising from the highly heterogeneous mixture of text, sequence tokens, and structure tokens) rather than genuine deterioration of the learned representations.

sections.title: 4 Instruction Tuning across Biological Tasks
sections.content: After multimodal continual pretraining, we perform supervised instruction tuning to align BioMatrix with broad spectrum of downstream biological tasks and to elicit its ability to follow natural-language instructions over heterogeneous biomolecular inputs and outputs. In this section, we first describe the task taxonomy and data sources of the instruction-tuning corpus (Section 4.1), then detail how

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Figure 4: Training loss curves of BioMatrix-1.7B and BioMatrix-4B during multimodal continual pretraining. Both models converge stably despite occasional transient loss spikes, which leave downstream performance essentially unaffected. heterogeneous datasets are unified into consistent instruction format with diversified prompts (Section 4.2), and finally report the training configuration (Section 4.3).

sections.title: 4.1 Task Taxonomy and Data Sources
sections.content: We curate comprehensive instruction-tuning corpus that spans three biological entity scopes molecule, protein, and interactionand, within each scope, covers both sequence-based and structurebased tasks. Table 3 summarizes the full task taxonomy, comprising 80 tasks drawn from diverse collection of public benchmarks; this taxonomy is used consistently throughout the paper, with the paragraphs below giving scope-level overview and Section 6, 7, and 8 reporting evaluation results organized along the same sub-task structure. Table 4 reports the overall training data statistics grouped by domain and modality, and detailed per-sub-task breakdown is further provided in Table 31. Molecular tasks. For molecules, we cover broad range of sequence-level tasks, including unconditional generation, name conversion, property prediction, captioning, text-based generation, forward and retrosynthesis, editing, customized generation, optimization, and question answering. For molecular structures, we include unconditional and property-conditioned conformer generation tasks. Protein tasks. For proteins, we cover sequence understanding, annotation prediction, knowledge mining, text-based protein design, and unconditional sequence generation. For protein structures, we include structure understanding, folding and inverse folding, sequencestructure co-generation, and unconditional structure generation. Interaction tasks. Beyond single-entity tasks, we additionally include moleculeprotein and protein protein interaction tasks in both sequence-only and structure-grounded forms, covering binding affinity prediction and interaction modeling at both levels.

Beyond the biology-specific tasks enumerated in Table 3, we additionally incorporate MegaScience [27] into the instruction-tuning mixture as source of general scientific reasoning supervision. Including this corpus helps preserve BioMatrixs broad scientific reasoning and instruction-following capabilities inherited from the base Qwen3 model, and prevents them from being eroded by the heavy concentration of domain-specific biomolecular tasks.

sections.title: 4.2 Data Format Unification and Prompt Diversification
sections.content: The collected datasets exhibit substantial heterogeneity in their original formats, and only fraction is natively distributed in instruction-style format. To bring them into uniform interface compatible with BioMatrix, we apply three transformations as follows. Modality serialization with special tokens. Whenever an input or output field contains biomolecular entity, we serialize it using the unified tokenization scheme and wrap it with the corresponding modality-specific start/end tokens, following the same protocol as in continual pretraining  (Table 2)  . Conversion to instruction format. For datasets that are not natively in instruction form, we manually design conversion pipelines that map each raw example into (instruction, input, response) triplet. Depending on the task type, classification labels are verbalized into natural-language category names,

Prompt template diversification. Using single fixed prompt per task tends to produce models that overfit to the surface form of the instruction and generalize poorly to paraphrased or unseen phrasings at inference time. To mitigate this, for each sub-task in Table 3 we manually curate pool of semantically equivalent prompt templates that vary in wording, sentence structure, and level of formality, while preserving the underlying task intent. Each training instance then samples one template uniformly at random from its task-specific pool to fill the instruction field.

sections.title: 4.3 Training Configuration
sections.content: Instruction tuning is also performed using the LLaMA-Factory framework [152]. We instruction-tune both BioMatrix-1.7B and BioMatrix-4B using the AdamW optimizer. The peak learning rate is set to 5.0 105 under cosine schedule with linear warmup over the first 10% of total steps. We use sample-level attention masking during training. The maximum sequence length is 2,048 tokens.

sections.title: 5 Evaluation Setup
sections.content: Our evaluation is designed to answer the two empirical questions: Q1: Feasibility of unified tokenization. Can biomolecular structures be discretized into tokens that share vocabulary with sequences and natural language, and decoded under single next-token objective, while still supporting high-quality structural generation, prediction, and understanding across both molecules and proteins? Q2: Competitiveness against task-specialized models. Does single unified backbonecovering molecules, proteins, and their interactions across sequence, structure, and text within one vocabulary match or surpass models that are purpose-built or purpose-trained for individual tasks? To answer these questions, we organize the evaluation along the same taxonomy used for instruction tuning  (Table 3)  : single-entity molecular tasks across sequence and structure settings (Section 6), singleentity protein tasks organized analogously (Section 7), and moleculeprotein as well as proteinprotein interaction tasks (Section 8). We emphasize that BioMatrix shares single tokenization scheme, single continually-pretrained backbone, and single next-token training objective across every result reported in this section. The instruction-tuning stage, in contrast, is organized into several task-group-specific variants whose

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: grouping is determined by data composition and per-task evaluation considerations. We make this division fully explicit in Table 30. Evaluated variants. All variants of BioMatrix evaluated in this section are instruction-tuned from the same continually-pretrained backbones and use the same unified tokenization scheme. The instructiontuning stage, however, is organized into task-group-specific variants summarized in Table 30, and the numbers reported correspond to these task-group variants. For practical convenience, we release single all-tasks merged SFT model trained on the union of all sub-task corpora with mild oversampling of small-data sub-tasks (see Section 9.1). Baselines. For each task, we compare BioMatrix against representative baselines drawn from the prior literature. The selected baselines span two complementary types of comparison: (a) generalist biological foundation models that, like BioMatrix, attempt to cover multiple task categories under shared framework (e.g., BioT5+ [96], NatureLM [137], SciReasoner [132], 3D-MoLM [63], ESM3 [42], ProtTeX [82]), against which we test whether BioMatrix advances the multi-task frontier within the unified-tokenization paradigm; and (b) task-specialized state-of-the-art models that represent the strongest published numbers on individual benchmarks (e.g., NExT-Mol [73], MolGPT [11], ESMFold [65], ProteinMPNN [23], Uni-Mol [153]), against which we test whether BioMatrixs task-group variants, derived from the unified backbone, can match dedicated specialists. The specific baselines considered for each task are listed alongside the corresponding results in Sections 68. We additionally report both BioMatrix-1.7B and BioMatrix-4B throughout to provide within-family scale comparison under an otherwise identical recipe. Evaluation protocol. For every sub-task in Table 3, we follow the official data split and evaluation metric provided by its source benchmark. All inference is performed with vLLM [57]. We adopt two decoding configurations depending on the nature of the task. For tasks whose evaluation does not involve output diversitysuch as property prediction, name conversion, annotation prediction, question answeringwe use greedy decoding with temperature 0 to obtain deterministic prediction. For tasks whose evaluation explicitly involves sampling diverse outputssuch as unconditional molecule generation or text-based protein designwe instead use nucleus sampling with top-p = 1.0, and adaptively tune the sampling temperature on per-task basis to balance fidelity and diversity according to the conventions of each benchmark.

sections.title: 6 Molecule Tasks
sections.content: We evaluate BioMatrix on molecular tasks spanning both sequence-based and structure-based settings, as summarized in Figure 5. Section 6.1 covers tasks that take or produce molecular line notations together with natural language, while Section 6.2 covers tasks that involve molecular geometry.

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Figure 5: Overview of the molecular task suite evaluated in this section. Key takeaways on molecule tasks. Strong parameter efficiency against larger LLM baselines. Across sequence-level molecular tasksspanning unconditional generation, name conversion, property prediction, captioning, text-based generation, forward and retrosynthesis, editing, customized generation, optimization, and question answeringBioMatrix achieves the best or second-best result on the vast majority of metrics among LLM-based methods, often surpassing baselines that are several times larger in parameter count. Native multimodality unlocks gains on cross-modal tasks. The improvements over prior specialists are most pronounced on tasks that explicitly bridge modalities or conditioning signals: text-based molecule generation, molecule captioning, and especially property-conditioned conformer generation, where BioMatrix reduces MAE on electronic-structure targets by roughly 34 over the strongest prior method. This pattern directly validates the value of placing sequence, structure, and text in single discrete token space. SMILES and SELFIES are complementary, not interchangeable. By natively supporting both notations under an otherwise identical recipe, BioMatrix reveals clean taskrepresentation match: SELFIES dominates tasks rewarding distribution-level chemical validity (unconditional generation, property optimization), while SMILES dominates tasks rewarding surface-level structural anchoring (customized generation, synthesis prediction). Maintaining both notations is therefore feature, not redundancy. Tokenization, not modeling capacity, bounds fine-grained geometric fidelity. On unconditional conformer generation, BioMatrix matches diffusion-based baselines on distributional and dihedral metrics but exhibits residual gap on bond-length precision. single lightweight MMFF refinement closes most of this gap, indicating that the limitation lies in the irreducible quantization error of finite structural codebooks rather than in next-token modeling itself.

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: 6.1 1D Molecular Tasks

sections.title: 6.1.1 Unconditional Generation
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to learn the underlying distribution of drug-like molecules purely from data, without any conditioning signal. The model is asked to generate 10,000 molecules from scratch as line-notation strings. We evaluate on two widely used benchmarks, MOSES [98] and GuacaMol [16], following their official protocols. Both benchmarks assess the generated set along three standard axes: validity, uniqueness, and novelty. MOSES additionally reports internal diversity (IntDiv1/IntDiv2), which measures the average pairwise dissimilarity among generated molecules via Tanimoto similarity over molecular fingerprints, with higher values indicating that the generated set spans broader range of distinct structures rather than collapsing onto similar ones. sections.title: 6.1.2 Name Conversion
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to translate between different molecular naming conventions, testing whether the model has internalized the bidirectional mapping between structured line notations (SMILES / SELFIES) and human-readable identifiers (IUPAC names, molecular formulas). Following the protocol of SMolInstruct [146], we evaluate on five sub-tasks that collectively probe both directions of this mapping: IUPAC-to-formula (I2F), IUPAC-to-sequence (I2S), sequence-to-formula (S2F), and sequence-to-IUPAC (S2I), where the sequence refers to SMILES for the SMILES variant of BioMatrix and SELFIES for the SELFIES variant. For each sub-task we report exact match accuracy (EM) as the primary metric, and additionally report chemical validity for I2S, which produces line-notation strings that must parse into legal molecules. As shown in Table 7, BioMatrix-4B establishes state-of-the-art performance across the board among LLM-based methods, achieving the best result on four of five metrics and remaining competitive on S2I EM with the strongest generalist baseline SciReasoner-8B while using roughly half the parameters. The improvement over SciReasoner-8B is most pronounced on I2S EM, rising from 84.40% to 92.83%. At the 1.7B scale, BioMatrix surpasses SciReasoner-1.7B on all EM metrics, indicating that the advantage is not simply function of model size.

sections.title: 6.1.3 Property Prediction
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to predict physicochemical and biological properties of molecules directly from their line-notation representations, testing whether the model has internalized the mapping from molecular structure to functional behavior. Following the protocol of SMolInstruct [146], we evaluate on six widely used MoleculeNet [136] benchmarks spanning two regression tasksESOL and Lipo, measured by root-mean-square error (RMSE)and four classification tasksBBBP, ClinTox, HIV, and SIDER, measured by accuracy. As shown in Table 8, BioMatrix achieves the best or second-best result among LLM-based methods on five of six benchmarks, and surpasses the task-specific literature SOTA on three of them (HIV, SIDER, and ESOL for the 1.7B variant). BioMatrix-1.7B already outperforms the much larger SciReasoner8B and BioMedGPT-Mol-8B on four of six benchmarks, again indicating that the advantage is not primarily driven by model scale. The four classification benchmarks (BBBP, ClinTox, HIV, SIDER) yield tightly clustered results across all four BioMatrix variants, indicating stable performance on these tasks. The two regression benchmarks show larger fluctuationsESOL RMSE in particular ranges from 0.810 to 1.740 across variants without clean patternwhich we attribute to the higher sensitivity of RMSE as continuous, unbounded metric compared to classification accuracy, rather than to systematic differences between variants.

sections.title: 6.1.4 Molecule Captioning and Text-based Molecule Generation
sections.content: These two tasks jointly evaluate the models bidirectional alignment between molecular structures and natural-language descriptions: captioning asks the model to produce textual description given molecule, while text-based generation asks it to produce molecule given textual description. Following the protocol of SMolInstruct [146], we evaluate captioning with METEOR against reference descriptions, and text-based generation with exact match accuracy (EM), fingerprint-based Tanimoto similarity (FTS) to the reference molecule, and chemical validity of the generated line notations. As shown in Table 9, BioMatrix achieves the best result among LLM-based methods on all four metrics

sections.title: 6.1.5 Forward Synthesis and Retrosynthesis
sections.content: These two tasks jointly evaluate the models understanding of chemical reactions in both directions: forward synthesis asks the model to predict the product given set of reactants, while retrosynthesis asks it to predict plausible reactants given target product. Following the protocol of SMolInstruct [146], we report exact match accuracy (EM), fingerprint-based Tanimoto similarity (FTS) to the reference molecule, and chemical validity of the generated line notations for both directions. sections.title: 6.1.6 Molecule Editing
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to perform localized structural modifications to given molecule under natural-language instructionadding, deleting, or substituting chemical component while leaving the rest of the molecule intact. It thereby probes finer-grained capability than text-based generation: the model must locate the target substructure, apply the requested edit, and emit chemically valid output that differs from the input only in the intended way. Following the protocol of S2-Bench [60], we evaluate on three sub-taskscomponent addition (AddComp), component deletion (DelComp), and component substitution (SubComp)and we report success rate (SR) and weighted success rate (WSR), where the latter additionally weights each successful edit by its structural similarity to the input molecule, so that high WSR requires the edit to be both successful and localized. As shown in Table 11, BioMatrix establishes new state-of-the-art results on AddComp and DelComp across both metrics. On AddComp, BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) reaches an SR of 0.9294 and WSR of 0.6447, improving over the strongest baseline Llama3.1-8B (OpenMolIns-xlarge) [60] by substantial margin on both axes despite using fewer than half the parameters. sections.title: 6.1.7 Customized Molecule Generation
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to generate molecules that satisfy explicit structural constraintsa specified atom count, bond count, or set of functional groupsrather than reproducing particular molecule described in free text. Compared to text-based molecule generation (Section 6.1.4), where each prompt describes single target molecule, customized generation specifies only structural recipe and admits any chemically valid molecule satisfying it. This shifts the task from one of imitation to one of constrained enumeration: the model must internalize what each constraint means at the structural level (e.g., counting atoms of given element, recognizing functional-group substructures) and then produce molecule that simultaneously satisfies the constraint and remains chemically valid. We follow the protocol of S2-Bench [60] and evaluate on three sub-tasksatom-number control (AtomNum), bond-number control (BondNum), and functional-group inclusion (FuncGroup)reporting success rate (SR) and weighted success rate (WSR), where the latter additionally weights each successful generation by its novelty against large external reference database (Zinc-250K [112]), so that high WSR requires the model to satisfy the constraints with structurally novel molecules. As shown in Table 12, BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) achieves the best result on AtomNum across both metrics, lifting AtomNum SR to 0.2774 and clearly surpassing every proprietary and open-source baseline. On BondNum, BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) reaches an SR of 0.1104, essentially matching the strongest baseline Llama3.1-8B (OpenMolIns-xlarge) [60] (0.1196) despite using roughly half the parameters. similar pattern holds on FuncGroup, where Llama3.1-8B retains the lead at SR 0.3548 and BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) reaches 0.2714, remaining competitive with the proprietary frontier models. The most striking observation is the large gap between the SMILES and SELFIES variants across all three sub-tasks: on FuncGroup, for example, BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) reaches an SR of 0.

sections.content: GPT-4o [50] GPT-4-turbo [92] Claude-3.5 [8] Gemini-1.5-pro [121] Llama3.1-8B (OpenMolIns-xlarge) [60] BioMatrix-1.7B (SMILES) BioMatrix-1.7B (SELFIES) BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) BioMatrix-4B (SELFIES) AtomNum SR WSR BondNum SR WSR FuncGroup SR WSR 0.1998 0.1702 0.1928 0.1742 0. 0.2176 0.0680 0.2774 0.0262 0.1339 0.1190 0.1335 0.1202 0.0811 0.1471 0.0492 0.1899 0.0184 0.0650 0.0774 0.1058 0.0708 0.1196 0.0996 0.0274 0.1104 0.0056 0.0412 0.0488 0.0697 0.0462 0. 0.0702 0.0178 0.0795 0.0041 0.2330 0.2180 0.2364 0.2486 0.3548 0.2408 0.0098 0.2714 0.0048 0.1518 0.1440 0.1556 0.1659 0.2268 0.1497 0.0062 0.1703 0.0032 whereas BioMatrix-4B (SELFIES) collapses to 0.0048. This pattern, which is largely absent from the imitation-style tasks evaluated earlier, points to structural mismatch between SELFIES and the kind of constraints this task imposes, and the mismatch manifests differently across the three sub-tasks. For AtomNum, the difficulty is that the number of atom tokens in SELFIES string does not in general equal the number of heavy atoms in the resulting molecule. SELFIES uses control tokens such as [Branch1] and [Ring1] that are each followed by one token whose role is not to contribute an atom, but to act as an index into the SELFIES alphabet that encodes the branch length or ring size. Concretely, the two SELFIES strings [C][C][Branch1][O][C][C][C] and [C][C][C][C][C][C][Ring1][N] each contain six [C] tokens, yet decode to pentane (CCCCC, five carbons) and cyclohexane (C1CCCCC1, six carbons) respectively: in the first string, the [C] token immediately following [Branch1] is consumed as branch-length index and contributes no atom, while in the second string the [N] token following [Ring1] encodes the ring size and likewise contributes no atom. model cannot therefore satisfy constraint such as contains six carbons by simply emitting six [C] tokens, whereas in SMILES every heavy-atom character is heavy atom. For BondNum, the difficulty is that the markers needed to count bonds in SELFIES are not standalone tokens the model can enumerate. In SMILES, non-single bonds appear as dedicated characters= for double and # for tripleand ring-closing bonds as matching pairs of digits, so checking constraint like contains two double bonds and one triple bond reduces to counting = and # characters. In SELFIES, by contrast, the same information is encoded in two ways that both obscure it. First, double and triple bonds are fused into the following atom token rather than existing as standalone tokens: acetylene is written C#C in SMILES (one #) but [C][#C] in SELFIES, with the triple-bond marker now baked into the second atom token. Second, ring-closing bonds are encoded through [Ring1] together with an index token specifying the ring size, and the bond type of the ring closure is itself carried as prefix on that same index token (e.g., the benzene fragment in benzoic acid ends with [Ring1][=Branch1], where [=Branch1] serves simultaneously as ring-size index and as an indicator that the closing bond is double bond). Counting bonds from SELFIES string therefore requires parsing the internal structure of several distinct token classes rather than aggregating over one, whereas in SMILES every non-default bond has dedicated, countable surface form. For FuncGroup, the difficulty lies in surface-form instability. Common functional groups in the benchmark pool (benzene rings, carboxyl groups, amides, and so on) correspond in SMILES to short, recurring substrings: every benzene ring is written as c1ccccc1 (or its kekulized variants), every carboxyl group as C(=O)O, every primary amide as C(=O)N, regardless of where they appear in the molecule. In SELFIES, by contrast, the same functional group can be split across discontiguous

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: token positions depending on its surrounding context. In benzoic acid (OC(=O)c1ccccc1), the benzene ring encodes into contiguous block [C][=C][C][=C][C][=C][Ring1][=Branch1]. In biphenyl (c1ccc(cc1)-c1ccccc1), however, the encoder chooses to realize the first benzene ring as ring whose tail is wrapped inside branch: the first four ring atoms appear as [C][=C][C][=C], followed by [Branch1][Branch1] control pair that opens branch containing the remaining two ring atoms and the ring-closing marker ([C][=C][Ring1][=Branch1]), after which the second benzene ring follows as its own contiguous block. The same chemical substructure (a benzene ring) therefore has no single canonical token signature in SELFIES, and model cannot anchor constraint such as contains benzene ring to one recognizable token pattern. SELFIES is great for open-ended generation, but this same feature makes it unsuitable for tasks with strict structural constraints. Across all three customized-generation sub-tasks, the SELFIES variants of BioMatrix collapse to near-zero success rates while the SMILES variants achieve stateof-the-art or competitive results. The underlying failure modesatom tokens reinterpreted as branch/ring indices, bond types folded into neighboring atom tokens, and substructures split across non-contiguous positionsall stem from SELFIESs context-dependent token semantics. This same property guarantees the syntactic validity that benefits unconditional generation (Section 6.1.1), but it prevents the model from anchoring structural constraints to recognizable token patterns.

sections.title: 6.1.8 Molecule Optimization
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to modify given molecule under natural-language instruction so as to improve one or more target properties while keeping the modified molecule structurally close to the original. Compared to molecule editing (Section 6.1.6), which specifies structural operation (add / delete / substitute component), optimization specifies property-level objective and leaves the model free to choose which substructures to modify in order to reach it. We evaluate BioMatrix on two complementary benchmarks: S2-Bench [60], which focuses on single-property optimization, and MolOpt [144], which additionally covers multi-property joint optimization. Single-property optimization. We follow the protocol of S2-Bench [60] and evaluate on three singleproperty optimization sub-tasks corresponding to three widely used molecular properties: octanolwater partition coefficient (logP), molar refractivity (MR), and quantitative estimate of drug-likeness (QED). For each sub-task, the model is given an input molecule together with an instruction specifying target property and the desired direction of change, and is asked to return modified molecule. We report success rate (SR) and weighted success rate (WSR), where the latter additionally weights each successful optimization by its structural similarity to the input molecule. As shown in Table 13, BioMatrix establishes new state-of-the-art performance on logP and MR across both metrics, with all four BioMatrix variants surpassing every proprietary and open-source baseline by clear margin. On QED, BioMatrix remains competitive with the strongest baseline Llama3.1-8B (OpenMolIns-xlarge) [60], with BioMatrix-4B (SMILES) trailing it slightly on both SR and WSR. Singleand multi-property optimization. We complement the S2-Bench evaluation with the MolOptInstructions benchmark [144], which extends molecule optimization in two directions: it enlarges the set of single-property objectives to cover drug-relevant attributes beyond logP/MR/QEDnamely QED, hydrogen bond acceptor count (acceptor+), hydrogen bond donor count (donor+), aqueous solubility (solubility+), bloodbrain barrier permeability (bbbp+), and hERG inhibition (herg-, where the goal is to decrease the value). It additionally introduces multi-property objectives (sol+&acc+, qed+&bbbp+) that require the model to optimize two attributes jointly. Following the original protocol of [144], each task is evaluated under two success criteria. Under the loose criterion (L), an optimization is counted as successful whenever the optimized property is shifted in the requested direction relative to the input. Under the strict criterion (S), the property must additionally clear per-property threshold

As shown in Table 14, BioMatrix-4B (SELFIES) achieves the best Correct and Validity scores on the majority of metrictask combinations under both criteria, with the remaining top positions filled by other BioMatrix variants. The improvement over the prior best baseline DrugAssist [144] is consistent across both singleand multi-property tasks, and is particularly visible on hERG inhibition (widely regarded as one of the hardest properties to optimize due to its complex and data-scarce structureactivity relationships) and on the strict solubility setting, the most demanding sub-task in the benchmark, where the strict criterion requires landing inside prescribed value range rather than merely shifting in the right direction. The advantage of BioMatrix on this benchmark also holds at the smaller scale: BioMatrix-1.7B (SELFIES) already surpasses every baseline on Correct for most tasks. The same SELFIES property that hurts customized generation helps property optimization. Across all eight MolOpt-Instructions sub-tasks and under both loose and strict success criteria, the SELFIES variants of BioMatrix dominate the Correct and Validity columns, with BioMatrix-4B (SELFIES) leading on the majority of metrictask combinations. This stands in direct contrast to the customized-generation results in Section 6.1.7, where the SELFIES variants collapsed to near-zero. The asymmetry reflects clean taskrepresentation match: optimization requires producing valid molecules with shifted properties, which SELFIES guarantees by construction and supports by abstracting away surface-form bookkeeping, whereas customized generation requires anchoring constraints to surface tokens, which SELFIESs context-dependent semantics actively obscure.

sections.title: 6.1.9 Molecule Question Answering
sections.content: This task evaluates the models factual accuracy in molecular knowledge, testing whether the model can correctly answer fine-grained questions about specific molecules rather than producing fluent but factually incorrect descriptions. We evaluate on MoleculeQA [78], the largest molecule-domain QA benchmark to date, which is designed to expose factual errors in molecular descriptions that traditional lexical-similarity metrics (e.g., BLEU, ROUGE) fail to detect. Following an MMLU [43]-style multiple-choice format, MoleculeQA covers four sub-categories of molecular knowledge: Structure, Source, Property, and Application, with overall accuracy (Total) reported as the primary metric.

As shown in Table 15, BioMatrix-4B establishes new state-of-the-art performance on the Total accuracy (73.78%) as well as on the Structure, Source, and Application sub-categories, substantially surpassing the prior best MolCA-1.3B (64.79%). We attribute this to the fact that property-related questions often require predicting over physicochemical attributes (e.g., precise solubility values or activity ranges), which is fundamentally different skill from the factual recall that dominates the other three sub-categories. 6.2 3D Molecular Tasks

sections.title: 6.2.1 Unconditional 3D Structure Generation
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to jointly generate chemically valid molecular graph and physically plausible conformer, without any conditioning signal. We evaluate on QM9-2014 [101] following the protocol of [73], reporting two sets of metrics that target the 2D graph and 3D geometry of the generated molecules, respectively. The 2D-level metrics comprise atom stability, molecule stability, and validity & completeness (V&C) for chemical validity; validity & uniqueness (V&U) and validity & uniqueness & novelty (V&U&N) for diversity; and similarity to nearest neighbor (SNN), fragment similarity (Frag), scaffold similarity (Scaf), and Frechet ChemNet Distance (FCD) for distributional similarity to the test set. The 3D-level metrics comprise atom stability and FCD computed on the 2D graphs reconstructed from the predicted coordinates, together with the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) of bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles against the reference distribution. Training-set statistics are also included as reference upper bound. Results are shown in Table 16. On the 2D side, BioMatrix delivers strong and well-rounded perfor-

Atom stability, molecule stability, and V&C all reach 1.000 / 0.988 / 1.000, on par with or above every baseline. The two axes where BioMatrix trails NExT-Mol are V&U&N (0.749 vs. 0.802) and SNN (0.495 vs. 0.530), both reflecting generated distribution that sits slightly closer to the training set than NExT-Mols. On the geometric side, BioMatrix shows more nuanced picture. In the default decoding setting, the raw conformers exhibit competitive dihedral-angle MMD but comparatively loose bond-length fidelity (MMD = 1.05) and lower atom stability (0.897). The bond-length MMD is highly sensitive to small shifts in the distributional mean, and we therefore interpret this number as indicating modest systematic offset in predicted bond lengths rather than catastrophic failure of geometry modeling. We attribute this primarily to two structural limitations of the MolStrucTok-based reconstruction pipeline. First, its 512-entry codebook must quantize four-dimensional continuous descriptor space, imposing an intrinsic quantization error on every per-atom geometric code that no amount of upstream modeling can recover. Second, coordinates are reconstructed autoregressively by sequentially traversing the molecule and rebuilding each local spherical frame atom-by-atom, which causes peratom decoding errors to accumulate along the traversal and occasionally produces geometrically implausible configurations; this stands in contrast to diffusion-based approaches such as NExT-Mol, which denoise all atomic coordinates jointly and therefore enjoy global geometric consistency that autoregressive reconstruction does not afford.

sections.title: BioMatrix
sections.content: Autoregressive structure reconstruction trades global geometric consistency for unification, but the gap is largely closable post-hoc. BioMatrixs raw conformers match diffusion-based baselines on dihedral-angle MMD but exhibit looser bond-length fidelity (MMD = 1.05) and lower atom stability (0.897) than NExT-Mol, reflecting irreducible quantization error from finite codebook compounded by per-atom error accumulation along the autoregressive traversal. single lightweight MMFF refinement step closes most of this gap (FCD: 1.04 0.23, atom stability: 0.897 0.985), indicating that the limitation is concentrated in fine-grained geometric consistency rather than chemical plausibility, and that combining unified token-level generator with cheap geometric post-processing is practical path to high-fidelity structural outputs.

sections.title: 6.2.2 Property-conditioned 3D Structure Generation
sections.content: This task evaluates the models ability to generate molecular conformers whose quantum-chemical properties match prescribed target value, testing whether model has internalized the mapping from molecular structure to physical observables rather than merely reproducing the training distribution. Following the protocol of [73], we condition generation on each of six quantum properties from QM9-2014 [101]: dipole moment µ, polarizability α, heat capacity Cv, HOMO energy εHOMO, LUMO energy εLUMO, and HOMOLUMO gap ε. To quantify controllability, we follow prior work [73] and partition the QM9-2014 training set into two disjoint halves of 50K molecules each, Da and Db: an auxiliary property predictor ϕc [44] is trained on Da and used to estimate the property values of generated molecules, while BioMatrix is trained on Db. We then report the mean absolute error (MAE) between the specified target value and the prediction of ϕc on each generated sample. Following [73], we additionally include ϕcs own MAE on Db as lower bound (L-Bound) that reflects the predictors intrinsic error and is unreachable by any generator.
